statement: Iceland faces persistent skills shortages in healthcare, construction, and technology. The healthcare sector had approximately 800 unfilled positions in 2025, with particular shortages in nursing, psychiatry, and geriatric care. Construction labour shortages have been estimated at 2,000–3,000 workers during peak building periods. The IT sector reports roughly 1,500 unfilled technical positions. EU membership would not change free movement rights (already guaranteed under EEA) but could improve mutual recognition of professional qualifications, particularly for regulated professions like medicine and engineering where EEA recognition processes are slower than intra-EU equivalents.
source_name: Vinnumálastofnun (Directorate of Labour) — Skills shortage reports 2025; SA — Labour market surveys
caveats: Skills shortage figures are estimates based on employer surveys and vacancy data, not census-level counts. Some shortages reflect wage levels and working conditions rather than absolute labour supply constraints.

related_entries.statement: Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.

EU/EEA nationals working in Iceland are concentrated in specific sectors. As of 2025, tourism and hospitality employed approximately 25–30% of EEA foreign workers, construction 20–25%, fish processing 10–15%, and healthcare 5–8%. Polish nationals are the largest single group, comprising roughly 35% of all EEA workers. Approximately 42,000 EEA/EU nationals held valid residence registrations in Iceland in 2025. The sectoral concentration has shifted over time: before 2015, fish processing and construction dominated, while the tourism boom (2015–2019) drew large numbers into accommodation, food services, and transport.
